suggestions.name: MapNumericConverter
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for converting map values to specific numeric types. They share common conversion logic, null-safety handling, and type checking patterns. Extracting them creates a focused converter class that separates numeric type conversion concerns from general map utilities.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: extracting numeric values from maps and converting them to specific numeric types (Byte, Double, Float, Integer, Long, Short). They all follow the same pattern - retrieve a value from a map using getNumber() as the foundation, then perform type-specific conversion with null safety and type checking.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "type conversion" domain, specifically focused on numeric type extraction and conversion from map values. They represent a cohesive subsystem for safely retrieving and converting numeric data from maps, handling both direct Number objects and String representations that can be parsed into numbers.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates numeric conversion logic from general map utilities
- Code Reusability: The converter can be used independently by any class needing numeric map value extraction
- Complexity Reduction: Removes 7 related methods from MapUtils, making it more focused
- Testability: Numeric conversion logic can be tested in isolation with comprehensive edge cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate type conversion concerns. The methods exhibit high cohesion (all perform numeric conversion from maps) and can operate with low coupling (minimal dependencies). This creates a focused converter that adheres to the Single Responsibility Principle.

suggestions.name: MapValueExtractor
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for safely extracting typed values from maps with default value fallback. They share the same core logic pattern and handle null-safety concerns while providing type conversion. Extracting them creates a focused, reusable component that separates safe map access concerns from general map utilities.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: safely extracting typed values from maps with default value fallback. They all follow the same pattern - take a map, a key, and a default value, then return either the converted value from the map or the provided default if the value is null or the map is null. The core logic is centralized in the `applyDefaultValue` method which handles the null-safety and default value application.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "safe map value extraction" domain. They represent a cohesive abstraction for type-safe map access with default values. This is essentially a specialized form of map value retrieval that handles type conversion and null-safety concerns. The methods work together to provide a complete API for extracting primitive and object types from maps safely.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates safe map value extraction logic from other map utilities
- Code Reusability: This extractor can be used independently by any class needing safe map access
- Complexity Reduction: Removes 20+ related methods from the main MapUtils class, making it more focused
- Testability: Easier to test type conversion and default value logic in isolation
- Maintainability: Changes to default value handling logic are centralized in one class

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate a cohesive set of related methods that handle a specific concern. The extraction creates a specialized component that follows the Single Responsibility Principle by focusing solely on safe value extraction with type conversion and default value handling.

suggestions.name: MapValueExtractor
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for safely extracting primitive values from maps with sophisticated fallback mechanisms. They share common extraction logic through the private `applyDefaultFunction` method and provide type-specific safe extraction for all primitive types. Extracting them creates a focused, reusable component that encapsulates the complexity of null-safe map value retrieval.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the primary responsibility of safely extracting primitive values from maps with fallback mechanisms. They all follow the same pattern: attempt to get a value from a map, apply a default function if the value is null, and return a type-specific default value as a final fallback. The core responsibility is type-safe value extraction with multiple fallback strategies.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "safe map value extraction" domain. They represent a cohesive subsystem for retrieving primitive values from maps while handling null cases gracefully. The shared `applyDefaultFunction` method serves as the common extraction algorithm, while each public method specializes it for specific primitive types (boolean, byte, double, float, int, long, short).

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates the complex logic of safe value extraction with fallbacks from other map utilities
- Code Reusability: This extraction pattern can be reused across different contexts beyond MapUtils
- Complexity Reduction: Removes 8 related methods and their shared private helper from the god class
- Testability: Allows focused testing of the fallback logic and type conversion behavior in isolation

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate a cohesive responsibility. The methods exhibit high cohesion (all perform safe value extraction) and can operate with low coupling (minimal dependencies on the original MapUtils class). This represents the Strategy pattern implementation for different primitive type extractions.

suggestions.name: MapValueExtractor
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for safely extracting typed values from maps with computed fallback behavior. They share the same algorithmic pattern and provide type-safe map access with intelligent default handling. Extracting them creates a focused class that encapsulates the complex logic of safe map value retrieval with functional defaults.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the primary responsibility of safely extracting typed values from maps with fallback behavior. They all follow the same pattern: attempt to retrieve and convert a value from a map using a key, and if that fails or returns null, invoke a default function to compute a fallback value. Each method handles a specific primitive or common type (Boolean, Byte, Double, Float, Integer, Long, Map, Number, Short, String).

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods represent the domain concept of "safe map value extraction with computed defaults." They form a cohesive subsystem for type-safe map access that eliminates null pointer exceptions and provides intelligent fallback mechanisms. Unlike simple default values, these methods accept functions that can compute context-aware defaults based on the original key.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates the complex logic of safe map access with functional defaults from other map utilities
- Code reusability: This extraction pattern can be reused across different contexts where safe map access is needed
- Complexity reduction: Removes this specialized concern from the broader MapUtils class, making both classes more focused
- Testability: The extracted class can be thoroughly tested in isolation, with clear test cases for each type conversion and default function scenario

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate a specialized concern (safe map value extraction with computed defaults) from a general utility class. The Strategy pattern is implicitly used through the Function parameters, allowing flexible default value computation. This extraction improves cohesion by grouping methods that share the same algorithmic pattern and data flow.

suggestions.name: MapOperations
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on enhanced Map operations including null-safe access, transformations, and detailed debugging output with circular reference detection. Extracting them creates a specialized class dedicated to Map utility operations, improving code organization and reusability while reducing the complexity of the original utility class.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the primary responsibility of providing safe, null-aware operations on Map objects and debugging/visualization capabilities. The cluster includes null-safe accessors (getObject, size), map transformations (invertMap, toMap), safe modification (safeAddToMap), and detailed printing functionality (debugPrint, verbosePrint with supporting methods). They collectively provide enhanced Map operations that go beyond the standard Map interface.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "Map operations" domain, specifically focusing on safe access patterns and debugging visualization. The printing methods (debugPrint, verbosePrint, verbosePrintInternal, printIndent) form a cohesive subsystem for Map visualization, while the other methods provide null-safe Map operations. They represent utility operations that enhance Map usability and debugging capabilities.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Separates Map enhancement operations from other utility functions
- Code reusability: These Map operations can be reused across different contexts that need safe Map handling
- Complexity reduction: Removes Map-specific logic from the larger utility class, making both classes more focused
- Testability: Map operations can be tested in isolation, especially the complex printing logic with circular reference detection

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate Map-specific utility operations. The extraction creates a cohesive unit focused on Map enhancement and debugging, following the Single Responsibility Principle by isolating Map-related concerns from other utility functions.
